g.data-narration: Welcome to Pipeline Architecture in Microcontrollers. This lecture explores how processors achieve high performance through instruction pipelining.
Pipelining is an implementation technique where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution, like an assembly line in manufacturing.
g.text: An implementation technique where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution.
Similar to an assembly line - different stages work on different instructions simultaneously.
Goal: Complete one instruction per clock cycle (CPI ≈ 1) vs 5 cycles without pipeline.

g.data-narration: Without pipelining, each instruction takes 5 clock cycles. With pipelining, we achieve near-one instruction per cycle throughput.
The MIPS 5-stage pipeline consists of: IF (Instruction Fetch), ID (Decode), EX (Execute), MEM (Memory), WB (Write Back).
The Instruction Fetch stage retrieves the instruction from memory using the Program Counter, then increments PC to point to the next instruction.
g.text: Key hardware: Instruction cache, PC register, Adders for PC+4

g.data-narration: In Instruction Decode, the opcode is analyzed, control signals are generated, and register operands are read from the register file.
g.text: Two read ports for source regs
Key hardware: Control unit, Register file (2 read, 1 write port), Sign extension unit

g.data-narration: The Execute stage performs ALU operations - arithmetic for R-type, address calculation for loads/stores, and branch target computation.
g.text: Key hardware: ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit), Branch comparator, Zero flag for branch decision

g.data-narration: In the Memory Access stage, data memory is accessed for load and store instructions. Other instructions simply pass through this stage.
g.text: Key hardware: Data cache, Memory address register, Memory data register

g.data-narration: The Write Back stage writes the computed result back to the register file, completing the instruction execution.
g.text: Key hardware: Register file write enable, Write data multiplexer, Destination register decoder

g.data-narration: Pipeline registers store intermediate results between stages: IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB. They enable simultaneous processing of multiple instructions.
g.text: These registers store intermediate results and control signals as instructions move between stages
Trade-off: Registers add latency but enable parallelism - net gain is massive throughput improvement

g.data-narration: Pipeline hazards are conditions that prevent the next instruction from executing in its designated cycle. There are three main types: structural, data, and control hazards.
g.text: Conditions that prevent the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle

g.data-narration: Data hazards occur when instructions depend on results not yet available. Three types: RAW (read after write), WAR, and WAW - only RAW is common in MIPS.
g.text: Note: WAR and WAW hazards occur in out-of-order execution. MIPS 5-stage is in-order, so only RAW is relevant.

g.data-narration: Data hazards are solved through data forwarding (routing results directly from pipeline registers) and pipeline stalling (inserting NOPs).
g.text: Route results directly from EX/MEM or MEM/WB
registers to EX stage of dependent instruction
Insert bubble/NOP until data is ready
Without forwarding: need 2 stall cycles. With forwarding: zero stalls - ALU result forwarded from EX/MEM to EX stage!

g.data-narration: Structural hazards occur when hardware can't support all instruction combinations. Control hazards arise from branches, solved via branch delay slots and prediction.
g.text: Example: Single memory port cannot serve
both IF and MEM in same cycle
Instruction after branch ALWAYS executes. Compiler can fill this slot with useful instruction!

g.data-narration: The theoretical speedup of an N-stage pipeline is N. For large instruction counts: Speedup = (I × N) / (I + N - 1) ≈ N.
